title: SEGMENT 8 — Abacavir and HLA-B\*5701: the prevent-toxicity test

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — Abacavir and HLA-B\*5701: the prevent-toxicity test
I want to switch gears now to a completely different kind of pharmacogenomics test.
Up to this point, we've been talking about variants in metabolic enzymes — chefs in the kitchen, faster or slower or absent.

The next two segments are about variants in a completely different system: the immune system, specifically HLA genes, and how variants in HLA can predict catastrophic immune reactions to particular drugs.
The first example is abacavir, a drug used for HIV.
Abacavir is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor.
It's part of the backbone of many combination HIV regimens.
Very effective.
Very well-tolerated, for most patients.

But about 5% of people who took it in the early years of its use developed something called a hypersensitivity reaction — a multi-system inflammatory syndrome that started a few days to a few weeks after starting the drug.
Fever, rash, gastrointestinal symptoms, sometimes respiratory symptoms.
If the patient kept taking the drug, the reaction could progress to a life-threatening, multi-organ syndrome.
If they stopped the drug and were re-exposed later, the reaction came back faster and worse — sometimes fatally.
So abacavir had this awful problem.
It was a very good drug for the 95% of people who could take it, and a potentially deadly drug for the 5% who couldn't, and you couldn't tell in advance which patient was which.
Clinicians would start abacavir and watch for the first signs of hypersensitivity, ready to stop the drug at the first hint.
It worked, mostly.
But it was nerve-wracking, and the 5% who reacted got sick, sometimes severely, before the drug could be stopped.
In 2002, a research group did a genome-wide association study comparing patients who had developed hypersensitivity to patients who had tolerated the drug.
And they found a single, dramatic association.
Almost every patient who had reacted to abacavir carried a particular variant of an HLA gene — HLA-B\*5701.
And almost no patient who hadn't reacted carried it.
The numbers, when properly tabulated, were extraordinary.
About 5% of European populations carry HLA-B\*5701.
Of those who do, the risk of hypersensitivity to abacavir is around 50% — a coin flip.
Of those who don't, the risk is essentially zero.
The variant accounted for nearly all the hypersensitivity cases.
Why does HLA-B\5701 cause this?
The mechanism, worked out subsequently, is fascinating.

HLA-B molecules are part of the immune system's antigen-presenting machinery — they hold up little peptide fragments for T cells to inspect, and if the T cell sees a peptide it considers foreign, it triggers an immune response.
The HLA-B\5701 variant has a binding groove with a particular shape, and abacavir, the small drug molecule itself, fits into that groove.
When abacavir occupies the groove, it changes the spectrum of peptides the molecule can hold, which causes the immune system to suddenly see normal self-peptides as foreign and attack them.
It's an autoimmune-like reaction driven by a small molecule altering antigen presentation.
Drug-induced T-cell activation by altered peptide repertoire.
A clinical trial called PREDICT-1 was run to test whether pretreatment HLA-B\5701 genotyping would reduce hypersensitivity.
The trial randomized patients to standard care or HLA-B\5701 screening before abacavir initiation.
The result was, by pharmacogenomics standards, spectacular.
The screened arm had zero confirmed hypersensitivity reactions.
The standard care arm had the expected ~5% rate.
A simple, cheap genetic test, done once before prescribing, eliminated a deadly adverse drug reaction entirely.
Since 2008, HLA-B\5701 testing has been the standard of care before prescribing abacavir, worldwide.
It is mandatory in many jurisdictions.
If you cannot demonstrate that your patient is HLA-B\5701 negative, you should not prescribe abacavir.
This is the prototype "prevent-toxicity" pharmacogenomics test.
It doesn't tell you about metabolism.
It doesn't tell you about dose.
It tells you, binary, whether a patient is at high risk of a specific catastrophic adverse reaction, and therefore whether to use the drug at all.
Positive: pick a different drug.
Negative: proceed with confidence.
Notice the value proposition.
The test costs maybe $50.
It prevents an adverse event that, when it happens, is severe enough to require hospitalization and can be fatal.
Even on pure cost grounds, before you weigh the human suffering, the test pays for itself many times over.
This is the cleanest cost-effectiveness case in pharmacogenomics.
The abacavir-HLA-B\*5701 story is the example I would pull out if a hospital CFO ever asked me whether pharmacogenomics testing was worth the money.
And it generalized.
The success with abacavir launched a search for other drug-HLA associations, and we found them.
Some are even more dramatic than abacavir.
